keyAchievements.description: He expanded the Sasanian Empire's territory into Roman Syria and Armenia and consolidated his father's conquests.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Cities
keyAchievements.description: He founded several major cities, including Bishapur and Gundeshapur, which became important cultural, administrative, and intellectual centers.

faq.question: What happened to the captured Emperor Valerian?
faq.answer: Historical accounts vary. Sasanian sources claim he was treated with respect, while Roman sources suggest he was humiliated, possibly used as a human footstool by Shapur. He died in captivity, and his skills, along with those of his soldiers, were used for construction projects in Persia.

faq.question: What was Shapur I's religious policy?
faq.answer: Shapur I was a Zoroastrian but practiced a policy of religious tolerance. He notably protected the prophet Mani, founder of Manichaeism, and allowed Christian and Jewish communities to thrive within his empire.

faq.question: What are the Naqsh-e Rostam reliefs?
faq.answer: They are a series of large rock reliefs carved into a cliffside in Iran. Shapur I commissioned a famous relief depicting his triumph over three Roman emperors: Gordian III, Philip the Arab, and the captured Valerian.

timeline.event: Born in Constantinople, son of Sultan Mustafa III.
Ascends to the Ottoman throne during the Austro-Turkish and Russo-Turkish wars.
Signs the Treaty of Jassy, ending the war with Russia and allowing him to focus on domestic reforms.
Formally establishes the 'Nizam-ı Cedid' (New Order) to modernize the state, starting with a new army.
Faces the French invasion of Ottoman Egypt and Syria, led by Napoleon Bonaparte.
Deposed by a Janissary revolt led by Kabakçı Mustafa, and his reforms are abolished.
Assassinated in the Topkapı Palace on the orders of his successor, Mustafa IV, during an attempt to restore him to the throne.

keyAchievements.title: Nizam-ı Cedid (New Order) Army
keyAchievements.description: Established a modern, European-trained infantry corps to challenge the power of the traditional Janissaries.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Modernization
keyAchievements.description: Opened the first permanent Ottoman embassies in major European capitals like London, Paris, and Vienna.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of Arts and Music
keyAchievements.description: Was an accomplished poet, calligrapher, and musician who composed many works, fostering a cultural flourishing during his reign.

faq.question: What was the Nizam-ı Cedid?
faq.answer: It was a series of reforms in the late 18th century aimed at modernizing the Ottoman Empire. Its centerpiece was the creation of a new Western-style army, but it also included fiscal, administrative, and diplomatic changes.

faq.question: Why did the Janissaries oppose Selim III?
faq.answer: They saw his new army, the Nizam-ı Cedid, as a direct threat to their centuries-old military dominance, political influence, and privileged status within the empire.

faq.question: Was Selim III's reign a complete failure?
faq.answer: While his reforms were overturned and he was killed, his efforts were the first major attempt at Western-style modernization. His reign is seen as a pivotal, though tragic, turning point that laid the groundwork for the subsequent Tanzimat reforms.

timeline.event: Born in Herat, modern-day Afghanistan.
Ascends the Safavid throne at the age of 16 amidst a military coup against his father.
Signs a harsh peace treaty with the Ottoman Empire, ceding large territories to buy time for internal reforms.
Moves the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan and defeats the Uzbeks near Herat, securing the eastern frontier.
Wages a successful war against the Ottoman Empire, reclaiming territories including Baghdad, Azerbaijan, and Georgia.
With the help of the English navy, captures the strategic island of Hormuz from the Portuguese.
Recaptures Baghdad from the Ottomans.
Dies in Mazandaran, leaving a powerful but unstable legacy due to the lack of a capable heir.

keyAchievements.title: Military Reform
keyAchievements.description: He created the 'ghulam' corps, a standing army of elite slave soldiers loyal only to the Shah, breaking the power of the tribal Qizilbash.

keyAchievements.title: Reconquest of Territories
keyAchievements.description: He successfully waged wars against the Ottoman Empire and the Uzbeks, restoring Persia's traditional borders.

keyAchievements.title: Isfahan's Golden Age
keyAchievements.description: He transformed Isfahan into a magnificent capital, a global center of art, architecture, and commerce, epitomized by Naqsh-e Jahan Square.

keyAchievements.title: Economic Prosperity
keyAchievements.description: He revitalized the Persian economy by monopolizing and promoting the silk trade, building infrastructure, and ensuring public safety on trade routes.

faq.question: Why is Abbas I known as 'the Great'?
faq.answer: He is called 'the Great' for restoring Persia's military and political strength, expanding its borders, and presiding over a golden age of culture and economy.

faq.question: What was his relationship with his sons?
faq.answer: Tragically, he grew paranoid of conspiracies. He had his eldest son and heir, Mohammad Baqer Mirza, executed and had two other sons blinded, leaving no capable successor to the throne.

faq.question: Who were the 'ghulams'?
faq.answer: The ghulams were an elite military corps composed of converted and trained Circassians, Georgians, and Armenians. They were fiercely loyal to the Shah personally, not to tribal or ethnic factions.

faq.question: How did he interact with Europe?
faq.answer: He actively sought alliances with European powers, such as Spain and England, to create a second front against their common enemy, the Ottoman Empire. This led to increased diplomatic and commercial contact between Persia and Europe.

timeline.event: Born in Nineveh, a younger son of King Esarhaddon.
Ascends to the throne of Assyria after the death of his father.
Conducts major campaigns in Egypt, culminating in the sack of Thebes.
A devastating civil war begins with his older brother, Shamash-shum-ukin, ruler of Babylon.
Defeats his brother and sacks the city of Babylon, ending the civil war.
Sacks the Elamite capital of Susa, effectively destroying the rival kingdom of Elam.
Dies, leaving the Assyrian Empire at its peak but severely overstretched and internally weakened.

keyAchievements.title: The Library of Ashurbanipal
keyAchievements.description: Assembled the first systematically collected and cataloged library in the ancient world, preserving over 30,000 cuneiform tablets.

keyAchievements.title: Consolidation of the Empire
keyAchievements.description: Maintained the Neo-Assyrian Empire at its peak territorial extent, stretching from the Caucasus Mountains to Egypt and from Cyprus to western Iran.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Elam
keyAchievements.description: Decisively defeated the long-time rival kingdom of Elam, sacking its capital Susa and ending its threat to Mesopotamia.

faq.question: Was Ashurbanipal really literate?
faq.answer: Yes. Uniquely for an Assyrian king of his time, he boasted in his inscriptions of his ability to read and write complex cuneiform scripts, including archaic Sumerian.

faq.question: What happened to the Assyrian Empire after his death?
faq.answer: The empire, weakened by decades of civil war and constant campaigning, rapidly declined and collapsed. Within two decades of his death, Nineveh was sacked and the empire ceased to exist.

faq.question: How was his library rediscovered?
faq.answer: The ruins of his library were discovered in the mid-19th century by British archaeologist Austen Henry Layard at the site of ancient Nineveh, in modern-day Iraq. The tablets found there are the foundation of modern Assyriology.

faq.question: What is his most famous depiction in art?
faq.answer: He is famously depicted in palace reliefs in a scene known as the 'Garden Party', where he reclines on a couch while the severed head of the Elamite king Teumman hangs from a nearby tree.

timeline.event: Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a wealthy family of mixed Turkish, Arab, Circassian, Kurdish, and Greek roots.
Graduated from the School of Law and was sent by Khedive Tewfik to study in France for three years.
Returned to Egypt and was appointed to a prominent position in the court of Khedive Abbas II.
Forced into exile by the British authorities due to his nationalist poetry and support for Egyptian independence. He chose to live in Andalusia, Spain.
Returned to Egypt from exile and was hailed as a national hero.
Crowned 'Amir al-Shu'ara' (The Prince of Poets) by his peers in a grand ceremony, acknowledging his status as the foremost poet in the Arab world.
Died in Cairo at the age of 64.

keyAchievements.title: Prince of Poets (Amir al-Shu'ara)
keyAchievements.description: Officially crowned by his peers in 1927, recognizing him as the greatest contemporary Arabic-language poet.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Arabic Poetic Drama
keyAchievements.description: He introduced and established the genre of poetic drama in modern Arabic literature with plays like 'The Death of Cleopatra' and 'Majnun Laila'.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Arabic Poetry
keyAchievements.description: He blended classical Arabic poetic forms with European influences, revitalizing the language and themes of Arabic poetry for the modern era.

keyAchievements.title: National Voice of Egypt
keyAchievements.description: His patriotic poems inspired the Egyptian nationalist movement and solidified his role as a cultural and political icon.

faq.question: Why was he called the 'Prince of Poets'?
faq.answer: The title 'Amir al-Shu'ara' was bestowed upon him in 1927 by a committee of Arab poets and writers to honor his unparalleled contributions, mastery of the Arabic language, and his role in reviving and modernizing Arabic poetry.

faq.question: What was the impact of his exile in Spain?
faq.answer: His exile in Andalusia (Spain) was a period of great creative output. It inspired some of his most powerful and nostalgic works, where he lamented his separation from Egypt and drew parallels with the lost glory of the Arab empire in Spain.

faq.question: What are his most famous works?
faq.answer: His most famous works include his collection of poems 'Al-Shawqiyyat', and his pioneering poetic plays such as 'The Death of Cleopatra', 'Majnun Laila', and 'Cambyses'.

faq.question: Is he still relevant today?
faq.answer: Yes, Shawqi is considered a foundational figure of modern Arabic literature. His poems are studied in schools across the Arab world, and his innovations in poetic drama and lyrical poetry continue to influence writers today.

timeline.event: Born in Lofça (now Lovech, Bulgaria).
Arrived in Constantinople to pursue higher education.
Appointed official historian of the Ottoman Empire.
Appointed head of the Mecelle Commission to codify Ottoman civil law.
Served his first of several terms as Minister of Justice.
Died in Constantinople.

keyAchievements.title: The Mecelle (Ottoman Civil Code)
keyAchievements.description: Led the commission that codified Hanafi law into the first systematic civil code of the Ottoman Empire, influencing law across the Muslim world.

keyAchievements.title: Tarih-i Cevdet (History of Cevdet)
keyAchievements.description: Authored a monumental 12-volume history of the Ottoman Empire from 1774 to 1826, a foundational text for modern Turkish historiography.

keyAchievements.title: Tanzimat Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Played a pivotal role as a statesman and intellectual in the 19th-century modernization reforms of the Ottoman state and society.

faq.question: What is the Mecelle?
faq.answer: The Mecelle (Mecelle-i Ahkâm-ı Adliyye) was the civil code of the Ottoman Empire in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It was the first attempt to codify a part of the Sharia-based law for a modern state.

faq.question: Was Ahmed Cevdet Pasha a conservative or a reformer?
faq.answer: He was a unique figure who sought a synthesis. He believed in adopting Western-style institutions but insisted they must be grounded in and compatible with Islamic tradition, making him a 'conservative reformer'.

faq.question: What was his most important contribution besides the Mecelle?
faq.answer: His 12-volume historical work, 'Tarih-i Cevdet', is considered a masterpiece of Ottoman historiography and a crucial source for understanding the empire's transition into the modern era.

timeline.event: Born in Ray, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Appointed chief physician of the hospital in Ray.
Moved to Baghdad and was appointed director of the main hospital, which he located using an experiment involving meat decomposition.
Wrote 'A Treatise on Smallpox and Measles' (Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasbah), the first text to clinically differentiate the two diseases.
Died in his hometown of Ray. His students later compiled his notes into the 'Kitab al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book).

keyAchievements.title: Differentiating Smallpox and Measles
keyAchievements.description: He was the first to provide a clear clinical description and distinction between the two infectious diseases.

keyAchievements.title: Kitab al-Hawi (The Comprehensive Book)
keyAchievements.description: Authored a monumental medical encyclopedia that synthesized Greek, Syrian, and early Arabic medical knowledge, influencing medicine for centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Experimental Medicine
keyAchievements.description: He used controlled experiments, such as hanging meat to determine the most sanitary location for a hospital in Baghdad.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Chemistry/Alchemy
keyAchievements.description: He systematically classified chemical substances, described numerous chemical processes, and designed and described over twenty laboratory instruments.

faq.question: Was Rhazes only a physician?
faq.answer: No, he was a polymath who wrote over 200 works on a wide range of subjects, including philosophy, alchemy, music, ethics, and theology, in addition to his extensive medical writings.

faq.question: How did he choose the location for the Baghdad hospital?
faq.answer: He famously hung pieces of fresh meat in various locations around the city and chose the spot where the meat decomposed the slowest, correctly deducing it was the most hygienic and least contaminated area.

faq.question: What was his most important medical book?
faq.answer: While his treatise on smallpox and measles was groundbreaking, his most extensive and influential work is the 'Kitab al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book), a massive encyclopedia of medical knowledge compiled from his notes after his death.

timeline.event: Born in Baghdad, son of the fifth Abbasid Caliph, Harun al-Rashid.
Haroen al-Rasjid overlijdt. De halfbroer van al-Ma'moen, al-Amin, wordt kalief in Bagdad, terwijl al-Ma'moen de oostelijke provincie Churasan bestuurt.
De Vierde Fitna, een burgeroorlog tussen al-Amin en al-Ma'moen, culmineert in het Beleg van Bagdad en de overwinning van al-Ma'moen.
Sticht het Huis van Wijsheid (Bayt al-Hikma) in Bagdad, een belangrijk centrum voor vertaling en onderzoek.
Sponsort een expeditie van astronomen om de omtrek van de aarde te meten, met een voor die tijd opmerkelijk nauwkeurig resultaat.
Begint de 'Mihna' (inquisitie) om de mu'tazilitische doctrine af te dwingen. Overlijdt tijdens een militaire campagne tegen het Byzantijnse Rijk.

keyAchievements.title: Oprichting van het Huis van Wijsheid
keyAchievements.description: Richtte een grote bibliotheek en academie op in Bagdad die het intellectuele hart van de islamitische gouden eeuw werden.

keyAchievements.title: De grote vertaalbeweging
keyAchievements.description: Sponsorde de vertaling van talloze Griekse, Perzische en Indiase wetenschappelijke en filosofische werken in het Arabisch.

keyAchievements.title: Mecenaat van de wetenschap
keyAchievements.description: Financierde grote wetenschappelijke projecten, waaronder astronomische waarnemingen en de eerste algebraïsche tekst van al-Chwarizmi.

faq.question: Wat was het Huis van Wijsheid?
faq.answer: Het was een belangrijke openbare academie, een intellectueel centrum en een bibliotheek in Bagdad tijdens de islamitische gouden eeuw. Geleerden met verschillende achtergronden kwamen daar samen om wereldwijde kennis te vertalen, te onderzoeken en uit te breiden.

faq.question: Waarom werd de regeerperiode van al-Ma'moen beschouwd als een 'gouden eeuw'?
faq.answer: Zijn actieve bescherming van wetenschap, filosofie, geneeskunde en kunst leidde tot een ongekende bloei van kennis en cultuur. Tijdens zijn regeerperiode werden grote stappen gezet in algebra, astronomie en geneeskunde, voortbouwend op de werken van oude beschavingen.

faq.question: Wat was de 'Mihna'?
faq.answer: De Mihna, of 'beproeving', was een inquisitie die in 833 door al-Ma'moen werd gestart om de mu'tazilitische denkschool op te leggen, die stelde dat de Koran geschapen was en niet eeuwig met God bestond. Hij vervolgde traditionalistische geleerden die het er niet mee eens waren, waarvan Ahmad ibn Hanbal de bekendste is.

timeline.event: Geboren in Bagdad, het Abbasidische Kalifaat, een belangrijk centrum van geleerdheid.
Begint aan zijn uitgebreide reizen, die meer dan twee decennia zouden duren en uitgestrekte regio's in Azië en Afrika zouden beslaan.
Reist door Perzië en naar de Indusvallei in India, waarbij hij lokale gewoonten en geschiedenis documenteert.
Voltooit de eerste versie van zijn belangrijkste werk, 《Muruj al-Dhahab》 (De gouden weiden).
Vestigt zich in Fustat (oud Caïro), Egypte, waar hij zijn latere jaren doorbrengt met het schrijven en herzien van zijn werken.
Overlijdt in Fustat, Egypte, met achterlating van een monumentaal maar grotendeels verloren gegaan oeuvre.

keyAchievements.title: De gouden weiden en mijnen van edelstenen
keyAchievements.description: Schreef dit encyclopedische werk dat geschiedenis, geografie en etnografie combineert, waarbij hij afweek van een puur chronologische naar een thematische benadering.

keyAchievements.title: Baanbrekende etnografie
keyAchievements.description: Documenteerde de gewoonten, overtuigingen en sociale structuren van diverse volkeren die hij ontmoette, op basis van directe waarneming en interviews.

keyAchievements.title: Integratie van wetenschappen
keyAchievements.description: Combineerde historische verhalen met wetenschappelijke discussies over geologie, zoölogie en astronomie, waardoor een holistisch wereldbeeld ontstond.

faq.question: Waarom wordt hij de 'Herodotus van de Arabieren' genoemd?
faq.answer: Net als de Griekse historicus Herodotus reisde al-Masoedi veel en schreef hij een veelzijdige geschiedenis met onder meer geografie, cultuur en persoonlijke waarnemingen, die verder ging dan eenvoudige kronieken van heersers en oorlogen.

faq.question: Hoeveel van zijn werk is bewaard gebleven?
faq.answer: Helaas heel weinig. Van zijn geschatte 34 werken zijn er slechts twee grote bewaard gebleven: 《De gouden weiden》 en 《Kitab al-Tanbih wa-l-Ishraf》. De rest, inclusief een universele geschiedenis van 30 delen, is verloren gegaan.

faq.question: Is hij echt naar China gereisd?
faq.answer: Hoewel hij uitgebreid over China schreef, wordt er onder geleerden over gedebatteerd of hij persoonlijk het vasteland van China heeft bezocht of zijn informatie heeft verzameld van zeelieden en kooplieden die hij ontmoette in havens in India, Sri Lanka en Zuidoost-Azië. Hij heeft zeker de Zuid-Chinese Zee bereikt.

timeline.event: Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Moved to Raqqa, Syria, and began his major astronomical observations.
Completed his magnum opus, the Kitāb az-Zīj aṣ-Ṣābi’ (The Sabian Astronomical Tables).
His work was first translated into Latin by Plato of Tivoli as 'De Motu Stellarum' (On the Motion of the Stars).
Died near Samarra, Iraq, while returning from a trip to Baghdad.

keyAchievements.title: Solar Year Calculation
keyAchievements.description: Calculated the solar year to be 365 days, 5 hours, 46 minutes, and 24 seconds, only 2 minutes and 22 seconds off the modern value.

keyAchievements.title: Trigonometric Innovations
keyAchievements.description: Introduced and popularized the use of sine, cosine, and tangent functions, replacing the Greek system of chords.

keyAchievements.title: Refining Precession
keyAchievements.description: Determined the precession of the equinoxes to be 1 degree every 66 years, a significant improvement on Ptolemy's value of 1 degree per 100 years.

keyAchievements.title: Kitāb az-Zīj
keyAchievements.description: Authored a comprehensive book of 57 astronomical tables that greatly influenced Islamic and European astronomy for centuries.

faq.question: Why is he called the 'Ptolemy of the Arabs'?
faq.answer: This title recognizes his immense stature in astronomy, comparable to Ptolemy in the ancient world, and highlights his role in significantly advancing and correcting Ptolemy's work within the Islamic tradition.

faq.question: Did Al-Battani use a telescope?
faq.answer: No, telescopes were not invented until the 17th century. Al-Battani made his remarkably accurate observations using instruments like the armillary sphere, mural quadrant, and astrolabe.

faq.question: How did his work reach Europe?
faq.answer: His major work, Kitāb az-Zīj, was translated into Latin in the 12th century as 'De Scientia Stellarum' or 'De Motu Stellarum', becoming a foundational text for Renaissance astronomers like Copernicus.

faq.question: What is a 'Zīj'?
faq.answer: A Zīj is an Islamic astronomical book that tabulates parameters used for astronomical calculations of the positions of the Sun, Moon, stars, and planets. Al-Battani's Zīj was one of the most accurate and influential of its time.

timeline.event: Born in Basra (modern-day Iraq).
Moves to Baghdad and begins to gain recognition in the intellectual circles of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Composes his major works, including the 'Book of Animals' (Kitab al-Hayawan) and the 'Book of Eloquence and Exposition' (Kitab al-Bayan wa-al-Tabyin).
Is briefly appointed to an office in the Caliph's court by al-Mutawakkil, but resigns after only three days.
Dies in his hometown of Basra at an advanced age.

keyAchievements.title: Book of Animals (Kitab al-Hayawan)
keyAchievements.description: A monumental work of zoology, literature, and philosophy, containing early concepts of natural selection and food chains.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Arabic Prose
keyAchievements.description: Elevated Arabic prose to a high art form, influencing generations of writers with his clear, witty, and anecdotal style.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering Polymath
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to a vast range of subjects including theology, sociology, politics, and literary criticism, embodying the spirit of the Islamic Golden Age.

faq.question: Did Al-Jahiz actually propose the theory of evolution?
faq.answer: He proposed concepts like the struggle for existence, adaptation, and environmental influence on animals, which are foundational to evolutionary theory, but he did not formulate a complete theory of evolution like Charles Darwin.

faq.question: What does the name 'Al-Jahiz' mean?
faq.answer: It is a nickname meaning 'the goggle-eyed' or 'the bug-eyed', reportedly given to him due to a congenital deformity of his eyes.

faq.question: How did Al-Jahiz die?
faq.answer: The exact cause is unknown, but a popular, though likely apocryphal, story claims he was killed when a pile of books fell on him in his private library.

timeline.event: Born in Kufa, Abbasid Caliphate, to an aristocratic family of the Kindah tribe.
Flourished in Baghdad under Caliph al-Ma'mun, working at the House of Wisdom to translate and study Greek philosophical and scientific texts.
Continued his scholarly work under the patronage of Caliph al-Mu'tasim.
Fell out of favor during the reign of Caliph al-Mutawakkil. He faced persecution, and his personal library was temporarily confiscated.
His library and favor were restored, allowing him to resume his work.
Died in Baghdad, leaving a legacy of over 260 written works.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Islamic Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: He was the first to systematically introduce and synthesize Greek philosophical concepts into the Islamic intellectual tradition.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Cryptanalysis
keyAchievements.description: He authored one of the earliest known books on cryptanalysis, introducing the method of frequency analysis to break ciphers.

keyAchievements.title: Polymath Contributions
keyAchievements.description: He wrote extensively on metaphysics, ethics, mathematics, medicine, music theory, and optics, demonstrating a vast range of knowledge.

keyAchievements.title: Music Theory
keyAchievements.description: He was the first great theoretician of music in the Arab-Islamic world and proposed adding a fifth string to the 'ud (lute).

faq.question: Wat was het Huis van Wijsheid?
faq.answer: It was a major intellectual center during the Islamic Golden Age in Baghdad. Scholars from various backgrounds gathered there to translate, study, and preserve knowledge from across the world, particularly from Greek, Persian, and Indian sources.

faq.question: Why was Al-Kindi's library confiscated?
faq.answer: It was likely due to a combination of court intrigues and theological disputes. Al-Kindi was associated with the rationalist Mu'tazilite school, which fell out of favor with the more orthodox Caliph al-Mutawakkil, and his rivals used this to discredit him.

faq.question: What is frequency analysis in cryptography?
faq.answer: It is a cryptanalytic technique, pioneered by Al-Kindi for code-breaking, that involves analyzing how often letters or groups of letters appear in a ciphertext. The method works because certain letters (like 'E' in English) appear more frequently than others in any given language.

faq.question: Did Al-Kindi only focus on Greek philosophy?
faq.answer: No, while he is famous for introducing Greek philosophy, he was a polymath who also made significant contributions to mathematics, medicine, optics, music theory, and was a pioneer in the field of cryptanalysis.

timeline.event: Born in Amol, Tabaristan (modern-day Mazandaran Province, Iran).
Left home at age 12 to study in Rayy, then traveled to Baghdad.
Traveled extensively through Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to collect hadith and historical reports.
Settled permanently in Baghdad, where he spent the rest of his life teaching and writing.
Completed his monumental history, 'Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk' (History of the Prophets and Kings).
Faced persecution from the Hanbali school in Baghdad, leading to a form of house arrest.
Died in Baghdad at the age of approximately 83-84.

keyAchievements.title: History of the Prophets and Kings
keyAchievements.description: A massive and detailed annalistic world history from creation to the year 915, preserving countless earlier sources.

keyAchievements.title: Tafsir al-Tabari
keyAchievements.description: One of the most comprehensive and influential early commentaries on the Quran, compiling a vast range of interpretations.

keyAchievements.title: Jariri Madhhab
keyAchievements.description: Founded his own, albeit short-lived, school of Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) that differed from the four main Sunni schools.

faq.question: Why is his 'History of the Prophets and Kings' so important?
faq.answer: It is one of the most comprehensive primary sources for the first three centuries of Islamic history, meticulously preserving reports and sources that would have otherwise been lost.

faq.question: What was al-Tabari's method as a historian?
faq.answer: He primarily used the isnad system, common in hadith studies. He cited his chain of transmitters for each historical report, allowing later scholars to assess the reliability of his sources.

faq.question: Why did his school of law (madhhab) fail to survive?
faq.answer: It faced fierce opposition from the more established and politically powerful Hanbali school in Baghdad, which disagreed with some of his legal interpretations and actively worked to suppress his influence.

faq.question: Was al-Tabari Sunni or Shia?
faq.answer: Al-Tabari is considered a Sunni scholar. While he founded his own school of jurisprudence, his theological and historical works are firmly within the Sunni tradition, though he is respected by scholars of all traditions for his immense erudition.

timeline.event: Born as Muhammad bin Dawud Chaghri.
Ascends to the throne of the Seljuk Empire after the death of his uncle, Tughril.
Captures Ani, the capital of Armenia, expanding Seljuk influence into the Caucasus.
Achieves a decisive victory over the Byzantine Empire at the Battle of Manzikert and captures Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes.
Mortally wounded by a prisoner during a campaign in Transoxiana and dies shortly after, succeeded by his son Malik-Shah I.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Manzikert (1071)
keyAchievements.description: Defeated a numerically superior Byzantine army, capturing Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes and opening Anatolia to Turkish migration.

keyAchievements.title: Consolidation of the Seljuk Empire
keyAchievements.description: Unified disparate Turkoman tribes and expanded the empire's territory, establishing it as the dominant power in the Middle East.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of Arts and Sciences
keyAchievements.description: Under the guidance of his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, he supported the establishment of 'Nizamiyya' schools, fostering an era of intellectual growth.

faq.question: Why is Alp Arslan called the 'Valiant Lion'?
faq.answer: Alp Arslan is a Turkic honorific name. 'Alp' means 'heroic' or 'brave,' and 'Arslan' means 'lion.' The name reflects his reputation as a fierce and courageous military leader.

faq.question: Did he intend to conquer Anatolia?
faq.answer: Most historians believe his primary goal was to secure his borders and counter the Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt. The victory at Manzikert was an unexpected opportunity that led to the gradual Turkification of Anatolia, rather than a premeditated invasion.

faq.question: How did Alp Arslan die?
faq.answer: During a campaign in Transoxiana, a captured fortress commander named Yussuf al-Khwarizmi, when brought before him for sentencing, lunged at the Sultan with a hidden dagger, mortally wounding him.

timeline.event: Born in Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) to a wealthy family with connections to the Ottoman court.
Reportedly has a dream of the Prophet Muhammad, which he interprets as a divine sign to embark on a life of travel.
Begins his first major journey outside of Constantinople, marking the start of four decades of extensive travel.
Travels extensively through the Ottoman Empire's territories in Anatolia, Rumelia, the Levant, Egypt, and neighboring lands including Persia, Austria, and Russia.
Dies, though the exact date and place are unknown. He is believed to have died in Constantinople or Cairo, having completed his 10-volume Seyahatname.

keyAchievements.title: The Seyahatname (Book of Travels)
keyAchievements.description: Authored a monumental 10-volume travelogue detailing 40 years of journeys, providing an invaluable window into the 17th-century Ottoman world.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering Ethnographer and Linguist
keyAchievements.description: Documented numerous languages, dialects, cultures, and social customs across a vast geographical area, many for the first time.

keyAchievements.title: Comprehensive Geographic and Social Record
keyAchievements.description: His work provides detailed descriptions of cities, architecture, trade routes, and social structures, making it a primary source for historians.

faq.question: Was everything in the Seyahatname true?
faq.answer: Not entirely. While it's a vital historical source, Evliya often embellished stories, included tall tales, and sometimes reported hearsay as fact, blending reality with imaginative storytelling.

faq.question: How did he fund his travels?
faq.answer: He was from a wealthy family and often traveled as part of official entourages for governors and pashas, serving as a companion, imam, or messenger, which provided him with protection and resources.

faq.question: How large is the Seyahatname?
faq.answer: It consists of ten large volumes. Each volume typically covers a specific period or geographical region of his travels, such as Volume I on Istanbul or Volume X on Egypt and Sudan.

timeline.event: Born in Kolozsvár (now Cluj-Napoca, Romania).
Captured during a conflict and brought to the Ottoman Empire, where he converted to Islam.
Enters the Müteferrika corps, serving as a diplomat and envoy for the Sultan.
Submits his memorandum, 'Vesiletü't-Tıbâa' (The Usefulness of Printing), to the Grand Vizier.
Receives a fatwa and an imperial edict from Sultan Ahmed III, authorizing the printing of non-religious works.
Publishes the first book from his press, the 'Vankulu Lügati', a two-volume Arabic-Turkish dictionary.
Publishes Katip Çelebi's 'Cihannüma', a major work of geography, including new maps he engraved himself.
Dies in Constantinople (Istanbul). His press had printed 17 different titles by this time.

keyAchievements.title: First Official Ottoman Printing Press
keyAchievements.description: He established the first printing press with movable Arabic type officially sanctioned by the Ottoman state in 1727.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Key Secular Works
keyAchievements.description: He printed 17 important works on history, geography, and language, including the famous 'Vankulu Lügati' dictionary and the 'Cihannüma' atlas.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Modern Cartography
keyAchievements.description: He engraved and printed some of the first modern-style maps in the Ottoman Empire, updating geographical knowledge.

faq.question: Why was printing with Arabic type introduced so late to the Ottoman Empire?
faq.answer: There was strong resistance from thousands of calligraphers who saw it as a threat to their livelihood, and religious conservatism that was wary of printing the Quran and other religious texts with a mechanical device.

faq.question: Was Muteferrika the very first to print in the Ottoman Empire?
faq.answer: No. Non-Muslim communities (Jews, Armenians, Greeks) had operated their own presses for centuries, but Muteferrika's was the first press run by a Muslim and officially sanctioned by the Sultan to print in the Ottoman Turkish language using Arabic script.

faq.question: What happened to his printing press after his death?
faq.answer: The press ceased operations shortly after his death in 1745 and was only revived several decades later, showing the personal drive and high-level patronage that was behind the initial venture.

timeline.event: Born in Kavala, Ottoman Rumelia (modern-day Greece).
Leads the Egyptian army in the Ottoman–Saudi War, defeating the First Saudi State.
Commands Egyptian forces in the Greek War of Independence, conquering the Morea.
His fleet is destroyed by a combined European force at the Battle of Navarino.
Leads the first Egyptian-Ottoman War, conquering Syria and advancing into Anatolia.
Decisively defeats the Ottoman army at the Battle of Konya.
Forced to withdraw from Syria by European powers under the Convention of London.
Becomes regent for his ailing father, but dies of tuberculosis after only a few months.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of the Egyptian Army
keyAchievements.description: Transformed the Egyptian military into a modern, European-style fighting force, making it one of the most powerful in the region.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest and Administration of Syria
keyAchievements.description: Successfully invaded and governed Greater Syria for nearly a decade, implementing significant administrative and legal reforms.

keyAchievements.title: Defeat of the First Saudi State
keyAchievements.description: Led the campaign that crushed the Wahhabi-led First Saudi State and recaptured the holy cities of Mecca and Medina for the Ottoman Sultan.

faq.question: Was Ibrahim Pasha the son or adopted son of Muhammad Ali?
faq.answer: While some early European sources claimed he was adopted, modern scholarship confirms he was Muhammad Ali's biological eldest son with his first wife.

faq.question: Why did he fail to take Constantinople in 1832?
faq.answer: After his victory at Konya, the path to the Ottoman capital was open, but diplomatic pressure and military intervention from Russia, followed by Britain and France, forced him to halt his advance.

faq.question: How did he die?
faq.answer: He died in November 1848 from tuberculosis, just a few months after being appointed regent and a year before the death of his father, Muhammad Ali.

timeline.event: Born in Córdoba, Al-Andalus (modern-day Spain).
Appointed qadi (judge) in Seville.
Became chief physician to the Almohad caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf in Marrakesh.
Fell from political favor, was exiled to Lucena, and his philosophical works were banned and burned.
Recalled from exile and his honor was restored shortly before his death.
Died in Marrakesh, Morocco.

keyAchievements.title: The Commentaries on Aristotle
keyAchievements.description: He wrote extensive commentaries that re-introduced and clarified Aristotle's works for Islamic, Jewish, and Christian thinkers.

keyAchievements.title: The Incoherence of the Incoherence
keyAchievements.description: A detailed rebuttal to Al-Ghazali's critique of philosophy, defending the use of reason and Aristotelianism within an Islamic context.

keyAchievements.title: Kulliyat fi al-Tibb (Generalities on Medicine)
keyAchievements.description: A comprehensive medical encyclopedia that was widely used as a textbook in Europe for centuries, known in Latin as 'Colliget'.

keyAchievements.title: Fasl al-Maqal (The Decisive Treatise)
keyAchievements.description: A philosophical treatise arguing for the harmony and compatibility between Islamic scripture and Aristotelian philosophy.

faq.question: Why was he called 'The Commentator'?
faq.answer: Dante Alighieri famously gave him this title in 'The Divine Comedy'. It was because his detailed analyses of Aristotle's works were considered the definitive interpretations in Europe for centuries, profoundly shaping medieval scholasticism.

faq.question: What is the 'double truth' theory often associated with him?
faq.answer: It's the idea that one truth can be reached through philosophy (reason) and another through religion (revelation), and both can be valid even if they conflict. Averroes himself argued for a single truth accessible through different paths, but some of his more radical Latin followers developed this controversial concept.

faq.question: Did he only write about philosophy?
faq.answer: No, he was a true polymath. He served as a respected judge (qadi) in Seville and Córdoba, was a court physician, and also wrote influential works on medicine, astronomy, mathematics, and Islamic jurisprudence.

timeline.event: Born near Bukhara, Transoxiana (present-day Uzbekistan).
Cures the Samanid emir Nuh ibn Mansur, gaining access to the royal library.
Begins a life of wandering after the fall of the Samanid dynasty.
Completes his major work, 'The Canon of Medicine' (Al-Qanun fi'l-Tibb).
Completes his philosophical encyclopedia, 'The Book of Healing' (Kitab al-Shifa).
Dies in Hamadan, Persia (present-day Iran).

keyAchievements.title: The Canon of Medicine
keyAchievements.description: Authored this five-volume medical encyclopedia which became a standard medical text in Europe and the Islamic world for centuries.

keyAchievements.title: The Book of Healing
keyAchievements.description: Wrote this monumental philosophical and scientific encyclopedia, covering logic, natural sciences, psychology, and metaphysics.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Aromatherapy
keyAchievements.description: Systematized the use of essential oils for therapeutic purposes, inventing the process of steam distillation.

keyAchievements.title: Synthesis of Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Integrated Aristotelianism with Islamic theology, creating a comprehensive philosophical system that influenced later Muslim and European thinkers.

faq.question: Was Avicenna just a doctor?
faq.answer: No, he was a true polymath, making significant contributions to philosophy, astronomy, logic, mathematics, and poetry, in addition to medicine.

faq.question: Is it true he memorized the Quran by age 10?
faq.answer: Historical accounts claim he was a child prodigy who had memorized the entire Quran by the age of 10.

faq.question: Why is he called Avicenna?
faq.answer: "Avicenna" is the Latinized version of his Arabic patronymic, "Ibn Sina" (Son of Sina).

timeline.event: Born in Murcia, al-Andalus (modern-day Spain).
His family moves to Seville, a major center of culture and learning.
Undergoes a profound spiritual conversion and begins his journey on the Sufi path.
Leaves the Iberian Peninsula permanently, beginning his extensive travels.
Arrives in Mecca, where he has a series of visions and begins writing his magnum opus, 'The Meccan Illuminations' (Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya).
Settles permanently in Damascus, which becomes his base for teaching and writing.
Completes 'The Bezels of Wisdom' (Fusus al-Hikam), a concise and highly influential work on his mystical philosophy.
Dies in Damascus and is buried at the foot of Mount Qasioun.

keyAchievements.title: The Meccan Illuminations (Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya)
keyAchievements.description: His magnum opus, a monumental encyclopedia of Islamic esoteric sciences, metaphysics, and cosmology.

keyAchievements.title: The Bezels of Wisdom (Fusus al-Hikam)
keyAchievements.description: A concise summary of his mystical teachings on the nature of prophets, considered a cornerstone of Sufi thought.

keyAchievements.title: Wahdat al-Wujud (The Unity of Being)
keyAchievements.description: Articulated the influential metaphysical doctrine that all existence is a single, unified reality, a manifestation of God.

faq.question: What does 'al-Shaykh al-Akbar' mean?
faq.answer: It means 'the Greatest Master' or 'the Greatest Sheikh' in Arabic. It is a title of reverence given to him by later generations of Sufis and scholars due to his immense influence.

faq.question: Waarom waren de leringen van Ibn Arabi controversieel?
faq.answer: Zijn doctrine van 'Wahdat al-Wujud' (Eenheid van Zijn) werd soms verkeerd geïnterpreteerd als pantheïsme (dat God het universum is), wat leidde tot beschuldigingen van ketterij door meer letterlijke of orthodoxe geleerden.

faq.question: Was Ibn Arabi een soenniet of een sjiit?
faq.answer: Hij wordt over het algemeen beschouwd als een soennitische moslim die in zijn jeugd de Zahiri-school voor rechtsgeleerdheid volgde. Zijn leringen zijn echter diep esoterisch en overstijgen vaak sektarische categorieën, waardoor ze denkers in zowel de soennitische als de sjiitische traditie beïnvloedden.

timeline.event: Geboren in Ardabil.
Duikt onder in Lahijan nadat zijn vader en oudere broer zijn vermoord.
Verovert Tabriz en wordt op 14-jarige leeftijd gekroond tot sjah van Azerbeidzjan.
Uitgeroepen tot sjah van Iran. Maakt het twaalver sjiisme tot staatsreligie.
Verslaat de Oezbeken in de Slag bij Merv, waarmee hij zijn oostgrenzen veiligstelt.
Lijdt een beslissende nederlaag tegen het Ottomaanse Rijk in de Slag bij Chaldiran.
Overlijdt op 36-jarige leeftijd.

keyAchievements.title: Stichting van de Safawidische dynastie
keyAchievements.description: Hij stichtte het Safawidische Rijk en herstelde een inheemse Iraanse dynastie die meer dan twee eeuwen zou regeren.

keyAchievements.title: Vaststellen van het twaalver sjiisme
keyAchievements.description: Hij maakte het twaalver sjiisme tot de officiële staatsreligie van Iran, een besluit dat de religieuze en nationale identiteit van Iran fundamenteel heeft gevormd.

keyAchievements.title: Vereniging van Iran
keyAchievements.description: Door middel van militaire campagnes bracht hij voor het eerst in eeuwen de ongelijksoortige gebieden van het Iraanse plateau onder één gecentraliseerd gezag.

faq.question: Waarom is Ismail I zo belangrijk voor de Iraanse geschiedenis?
faq.answer: Hij stichtte de Safawidische dynastie, die het begin markeerde van de moderne Iraanse geschiedenis, verenigde het land en vestigde de sjiitische identiteit die Iran tot op de dag van vandaag kenmerkt.

faq.question: Wat was de Qizilbash?
faq.answer: De Qizilbash ('Roodkoppen') waren een coalitie van loyale Turkmeense stammen die vrome volgelingen waren van de Safawiyya-orde en de ruggengraat vormden van Ismails leger.

faq.question: Wat gebeurde er na de Slag bij Chaldiran?
faq.answer: De nederlaag verbrijzelde Ismails geloof in zijn eigen onoverwinnelijkheid. Hij leidde nooit meer persoonlijk een leger in de strijd, trok zich terug uit het actieve bestuur en zocht naar verluidt zijn toevlucht in de drank.

timeline.event: Geboorte van Zarathustra in het noordoosten van Iran of omliggende gebieden. De exacte datum en locatie worden betwist onder geleerden.
Ontvangt zijn eerste openbaring van Ahura Mazda en de Amesha Spenta's (goddelijke entiteiten) tijdens een ritueel aan de rivier.
Brengt een decennium door met het prediken van zijn nieuwe visie met weinig succes, geconfronteerd met vervolging en afwijzing door zijn gemeenschap.
Reist naar het hof van koning Vishtaspa in Bactrië en bekeert hem met succes, waardoor hij een machtige beschermheer voor zijn geloof krijgt.
Dood van Zarathustra. De traditie wil dat hij werd vermoord door een rivaliserende priester tijdens het bidden in een vuurtempel.

keyAchievements.title: Stichting van het zoroastrisme
keyAchievements.description: Hij stichtte een van 's werelds eerste geopenbaarde, monotheïstische religies, gecentreerd rond de ene wijze god, Ahura Mazda.

keyAchievements.title: Ethisch dualisme
keyAchievements.description: Hij introduceerde het concept van een kosmische strijd tussen goed (Asha) en kwaad (Druj), waardoor de mensheid een vrije wil kreeg om hun kant te kiezen.

keyAchievements.title: Invloed op abrahamitische religies
keyAchievements.description: Zijn ideeën over monotheïsme, hemel, hel, een laatste oordeel en een verlosserfiguur hebben naar verluidt invloed gehad op het jodendom, het christendom en de islam.

faq.question: Is Zarathustra dezelfde als Zoroaster?
faq.answer: Ja. Zarathustra is de naam in de Avestische taal, terwijl Zoroaster de Griekse transliteratie is, die gebruikelijker werd in westerse teksten.

faq.question: Was het zoroastrisme de eerste monotheïstische religie?
faq.answer: Het wordt beschouwd als een van de vroegste, zo niet de eerste, geopenbaarde monotheïstische religies. De ontwikkeling ervan dateert van vóór of is gelijktijdig met het monotheïsme van Achnaton in Egypte en het vroege jodendom.

faq.question: Wat zijn de Gatha's?
faq.answer: De Gatha's zijn een verzameling van 17 hymnen, waarvan wordt aangenomen dat ze de woorden van Zarathustra zelf zijn. Ze vormen de kern van de zoroastrische heilige tekst, de Avesta.

timeline.event: Born in Asadabad, with his exact birthplace (Iran or Afghanistan) being a subject of historical debate.
Becomes an advisor to the ruler of Afghanistan, marking his early involvement in state politics.
Resides in Egypt, where he influences a generation of intellectuals, including Muhammad Abduh, before being exiled due to British pressure.
Co-founds the anti-colonial journal 'Al-Urwah al-Wuthqa' (The Firmest Bond) in Paris with Muhammad Abduh.
Active in Persia (Iran), his agitation against the Shah's tobacco concession to Britain leads to his expulsion and helps spark the Tobacco Protest.
Invited to Istanbul by Sultan Abdul Hamid II, where he remains under the Sultan's patronage and surveillance.
Dies of cancer in Istanbul. His remains were later moved to Afghanistan in 1944.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Pan-Islamism
keyAchievements.description: He was a leading advocate for the political unity of all Muslims under one leader to resist European colonialism.

keyAchievements.title: Islamic Modernism
keyAchievements.description: He argued for a reinterpretation of Islam that embraced rationalism and modern science to revitalize Muslim societies.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-Imperialist Activism
keyAchievements.description: Through his writings and speeches across the Muslim world, he inspired numerous anti-colonial movements and nationalist sentiments.

faq.question: Was al-Afghani from Afghanistan or Iran?
faq.answer: His birthplace is disputed. He was likely born in Asadabad, Iran, to a Shia family, but he claimed to be from Asadabad, Afghanistan, and of Sunni background to have broader appeal in the predominantly Sunni Muslim world.

faq.question: What was 'Al-Urwah al-Wuthqa'?
faq.answer: It was a short-lived but highly influential anti-colonial journal he published with Muhammad Abduh in Paris. It called for Islamic unity, reform, and resistance against European powers and was banned in British-controlled territories like Egypt and India.

faq.question: What was his relationship with Sultan Abdul Hamid II?
faq.answer: The Sultan invited him to Istanbul to use his Pan-Islamic influence for the Ottoman Empire's benefit. However, the relationship was complex; the Sultan grew wary of al-Afghani's radicalism and kept him under virtual house arrest until his death.

timeline.event: Born in Tus, Khorasan (modern-day Iran).
Active in Baghdad under the patronage of the Barmakid family, viziers to Caliph Harun al-Rashid.
The Barmakid family falls from power. Jabir is reportedly placed under house arrest in Kufa.
Dies in Kufa (in modern-day Iraq).
His works, under the Latinized name 'Geber,' are translated and begin to heavily influence European alchemy and science.

keyAchievements.title: Systematic Experimentation
keyAchievements.description: He championed a rigorous experimental approach to alchemy, laying the foundation for modern chemistry.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Apparatus
keyAchievements.description: He is credited with inventing or perfecting the alembic still, crucial for distillation, and many other laboratory tools.

keyAchievements.title: Chemical Synthesis
keyAchievements.description: He provided the first known recipes for synthesizing mineral acids like nitric acid, sulfuric acid, and aqua regia.

keyAchievements.title: Classification of Substances
keyAchievements.description: He developed one of the earliest systems for classifying chemical substances based on their properties.

faq.question: Is Jabir ibn Hayyan a real historical figure?
faq.answer: Yes, he is considered a real person. However, the vast collection of works attributed to him, the 'Jabirian corpus,' was likely written by him and a school of his followers over more than a century.

faq.question: Was he an alchemist or a chemist?
faq.answer: He was a transitional figure. While he worked within the alchemical tradition (e.g., seeking the philosopher's stone), his insistence on systematic, repeatable experiments makes him a foundational figure for modern chemistry.

faq.question: What is the 'Jabirian problem'?
faq.answer: It's the scholarly debate over the authorship of the thousands of texts attributed to Jabir. It is difficult to distinguish his authentic works from those written later by his followers in his name.

faq.question: What was his most important invention?
faq.answer: The alembic still is arguably his most impactful invention. It revolutionized distillation, a fundamental process for separating liquids that is still central to chemistry and industry today.

timeline.event: Born in Diyarbakır, Ottoman Empire.
Moves to Istanbul to study and becomes involved with the revolutionary Young Turk movement.
Becomes a leading member of the Committee of Union and Progress (CUP) in Salonica, the movement's intellectual hub.
Begins publishing his influential articles on sociology and Turkism, shaping the movement's ideology.
Arrested by British occupation forces and exiled to Malta along with other Ottoman nationalist leaders.
Released from exile and joins the Turkish nationalist movement in Ankara during the War of Independence.
Publishes his seminal work, 'The Principles of Turkism' (Türkçülüğün Esasları), codifying his ideology.
Dies in Istanbul, shortly after the founding of the Republic of Turkey, which was heavily influenced by his ideas.

keyAchievements.title: Systematization of Turkish Nationalism
keyAchievements.description: He is widely regarded as the 'father of Turkish nationalism' for synthesizing various Turkic ideas into a coherent ideology known as Turkism.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Atatürk's Reforms
keyAchievements.description: His ideas on secularism, populism, and nationalism provided the direct intellectual framework for many of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's foundational reforms.

keyAchievements.title: Distinction between Culture and Civilization
keyAchievements.description: He developed a key sociological concept distinguishing 'culture' (national and spiritual) from 'civilization' (international and material), guiding Turkey's path to modernization.

faq.question: Was Ziya Gökalp religious?
faq.answer: While he was a strong advocate for a secular state where religion was separate from politics and law, he viewed Islam as a vital and inseparable component of Turkish national culture and identity.

faq.question: What was his relationship with Atatürk?
faq.answer: Gökalp was a major intellectual influence on Atatürk. Atatürk adopted and implemented many of Gökalp's ideas on nationalism, secularism, and modernization when establishing the Republic of Turkey.

faq.question: What is 'Turkism' according to Gökalp?
faq.answer: Turkism, as defined by Gökalp, is an ideology promoting the cultural and political solidarity of Turkish peoples. It emphasizes a shared language, history, and culture—not race—as the foundation of the nation.

timeline.event: Born in the village of Pari, near modern-day Malayer, to the Zand tribe.
Nader Shah is assassinated, plunging Iran into civil war. Karim Khan returns to his homeland and begins to consolidate power.
Becomes a major contender for power in western Iran after forming a tripartite alliance.
Establishes Shiraz as his capital after defeating all major rivals and controlling most of Iran. He adopts the title Vakil ol-Ro'aya (Regent of the People).
His reign is marked by peace and prosperity. He commissions major construction projects in Shiraz, including the Arg of Karim Khan, Vakil Bazaar, and Vakil Mosque.
Dies of natural causes in Shiraz. His death triggers succession struggles that lead to the downfall of the Zand dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Vereniging van Iran
keyAchievements.description: He reunified most of Iran after the chaotic period of civil war that followed Nader Shah's death.

keyAchievements.title: Era of Peace and Prosperity
keyAchievements.description: His nearly 30-year reign is remembered as a period of exceptional calm, justice, and economic growth for the Iranian people.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of Arts and Architecture
keyAchievements.description: He transformed his capital, Shiraz, with magnificent architectural projects and fostered a flourishing of arts and culture.

faq.question: Why did he refuse the title of 'Shah' (King)?
faq.answer: He preferred the title 'Vakil ol-Ro'aya' (Regent of the People) to show that his power derived from and was exercised on behalf of his subjects, emphasizing a more humble and just form of rule.

faq.question: What is his most famous building?
faq.answer: The Arg of Karim Khan, a large citadel located in the center of Shiraz, which served as his living quarters and military command center.

faq.question: How did the Zand dynasty end?
faq.answer: After Karim Khan's death, his incompetent and feuding successors weakened the state, allowing Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar to conquer their territories and establish the Qajar dynasty.

timeline.event: Born in Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul).
Appointed as a clerk in the imperial army's Anatolian financial bureaucracy.
Participated in Ottoman campaigns against the Safavids in Persia, traveling to Baghdad and Hamadan.
Inherited a large fortune, which allowed him to leave his bureaucratic post and pursue a life of scholarship.
Began compiling his monumental bibliographic work, 'Kashf al-Zunun' (The Removal of Doubt from the Names of Books and Arts).
Began writing his major geographical work, 'Jihannuma' (Mirror of the World).
Died in Constantinople, leaving several of his major works to be completed by others.

keyAchievements.title: Kashf al-Zunun
keyAchievements.description: Authored a massive bibliographic encyclopedia listing over 14,500 books and 9,500 authors in Arabic, Persian, and Turkish.

keyAchievements.title: Jihannuma
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a groundbreaking universal geography that integrated Islamic and European sources, a first in the Ottoman world.

keyAchievements.title: Fezleke
keyAchievements.description: Compiled a comprehensive chronicle of Ottoman history from 1592 to 1655, serving as a key historical source.

keyAchievements.title: Mizan al-Haqq
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'The Balance of Truth,' a collection of essays on controversial religious and ethical topics, advocating for a more rational approach.

faq.question: Why is he called Katip Çelebi?
faq.answer: 'Katip' means 'scribe' or 'clerk,' reflecting his early career in the Ottoman bureaucracy. 'Çelebi' was an honorific title meaning 'gentleman' or 'man of God.' His real name was Mustafa ibn Abdullah.

faq.question: What makes his work 'Kashf al-Zunun' so important?
faq.answer: It is one of the most comprehensive bibliographic works of the pre-modern era, providing invaluable information on thousands of books and authors, many of which are now lost or rare.

faq.question: Was Katip Çelebi only a historian?
faq.answer: No, he was a true polymath. He wrote extensively on geography, bibliography, ethics, religion, and science, attempting to synthesize all available knowledge of his time.

faq.question: How did his military service influence his work?
faq.answer: His travels during military campaigns exposed him to different regions, cultures, and libraries across the empire. This firsthand experience, combined with the loss he witnessed, fueled his desire to preserve and organize knowledge for future generations.

timeline.event: Born to King Darius I and Atossa, a daughter of Cyrus the Great.
Ascends to the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after the death of his father, Darius I.
Crushes a major revolt in Egypt, asserting his authority.
Violently suppresses a revolt in Babylon, confiscating the sacred statue of Marduk.
Launches a massive invasion of Greece. Wins the Battle of Thermopylae, sacks Athens, but suffers a decisive naval defeat at the Battle of Salamis.
The Persian army left in Greece under his general Mardonius is decisively defeated at the Battle of Plataea, ending the invasion.
Focuses on ambitious construction projects, notably at Persepolis and Susa, completing many buildings his father had started.
Assassinated in a palace conspiracy led by Artabanus, the commander of his royal bodyguard.

keyAchievements.title: Suppression of Revolts
keyAchievements.description: Successfully quelled major rebellions in Egypt and Babylon early in his reign, securing the stability of the empire.

keyAchievements.title: Invasion of Greece
keyAchievements.description: Assembled one of the largest multinational armies of the ancient world for his campaign against the Greek city-states.

keyAchievements.title: Grand Construction Projects
keyAchievements.description: Completed many of his father's building projects and initiated his own, significantly expanding the magnificent city of Persepolis.

faq.question: Why is Xerxes often portrayed as a villain or a tyrant?
faq.answer: Most surviving historical accounts of his invasion of Greece were written by the victorious Greeks (like Herodotus), who naturally depicted him as a tyrannical and hubristic antagonist to glorify their own victory.

faq.question: Did Xerxes really have a bridge of boats built across the Hellespont?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a well-documented engineering feat. His engineers yoked together hundreds of ships to create two pontoon bridges, allowing his massive army to cross from Asia into Europe.

faq.question: What was Xerxes's religion?
faq.answer: Xerxes was a follower of Zoroastrianism, the state religion of the Achaemenid Empire, which centered on the worship of a single supreme god, Ahura Mazda. He references Ahura Mazda in his royal inscriptions.

faq.question: How did Xerxes die?
faq.answer: He was assassinated in 465 BCE in his bedchamber as part of a palace conspiracy. The plot was likely orchestrated by Artabanus, the commander of his bodyguard, who then briefly held power before being overthrown by Xerxes's son, Artaxerxes I.

timeline.event: Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Encounters Muhammad ibn Musa ibn Shakir, who invites him to Baghdad to work as a scholar.
Joins the circle of scholars in Baghdad, working under the patronage of the Banu Musa and later the Caliph al-Mu'tadid.
Produces major translations of Greek works (Euclid's Elements, Ptolemy's Almagest) and original treatises on mathematics, astronomy, and mechanics.
Dies in Baghdad, leaving a legacy carried on by his son, Sinan, and grandson, Ibrahim.

keyAchievements.title: Translation and Preservation of Greek Science
keyAchievements.description: Translated foundational Greek texts by Euclid, Archimedes, Apollonius, and Ptolemy into Arabic, making them accessible to the Islamic world.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Trepidation
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a theory of the oscillation of the equinoxes to account for perceived variations in the precession rate.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Number Theory
keyAchievements.description: Discovered a theorem for finding pairs of amicable numbers, extending the work of the ancient Greeks.

keyAchievements.title: Foundations of Statics
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Kitab fi'l-qarastun' (The Book of the Steelyard), a fundamental work on mechanics and the principles of levers and equilibrium.

faq.question: What religion did Thabit ibn Qurra follow?
faq.answer: He was a member of the Sabian religious sect from Harran, a Hellenistic pagan group that revered stars and celestial bodies. This often caused friction with orthodox Muslim authorities.

faq.question: What are amicable numbers?
faq.answer: Amicable numbers are two different numbers so related that the sum of the proper divisors of each is equal to the other number. Thabit's rule is a generalization that helped find such pairs.

faq.question: Was his theory of trepidation correct?
faq.answer: No, the theory of trepidation was later superseded by a more accurate understanding of uniform precession. However, it was an important and influential attempt to explain observational discrepancies of his time.

timeline.event: Born Fátimih Baraghání in Qazvin, Persia.
Becomes one of the first followers of the Báb, recognized as the 17th Letter of the Living.
Attends the Conference of Badasht, where she removes her veil, symbolizing a break with the past.
Placed under house arrest due to her influential teachings.
Executed by strangulation in a garden in Tehran for her Bábí faith.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering Feminist Icon
keyAchievements.description: Became one of the first major advocates for women's rights in the Middle East, challenging religious and social norms.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Bábí Theologian
keyAchievements.description: Was the only woman designated as a 'Letter of the Living,' the Báb's first disciples, shaping early Bábí doctrine.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Poet
keyAchievements.description: Authored powerful mystical and revolutionary poetry that continues to inspire readers and activists today.

faq.question: Why is she called Tahirih?
faq.answer: The title 'Tahirih' (meaning 'The Pure One') was bestowed upon her at the Conference of Badasht, recognizing her spiritual station and purity of spirit. Sources attribute the bestowing of the title to either the Báb or Baháʼu'lláh.

faq.question: Did she ever meet the Báb in person?
faq.answer: No, she never met the Báb. Their entire connection was through letters and the accounts of other followers, yet she became one of his most prominent disciples.

faq.question: What was the significance of her unveiling?
faq.answer: In 19th-century Persia, a woman appearing unveiled before men was a radical and shocking act. It symbolized a deliberate break from Islamic law and tradition, heralding the new spiritual and social laws of the Bábí dispensation, which included greater equality between men and women.

timeline.event: Born as Muhammad bin Suleyman, likely in the region of modern-day Iraq (Karbala or Hillah).
Receives a comprehensive education, mastering Azerbaijani, Persian, and Arabic, along with theology and mathematics.
The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent captures Baghdad from the Safavids, shifting the political landscape of Fuzûlî's life.
Composes his masterpiece, the romantic epic 'Leylâ vü Mecnûn' (Layla and Majnun) in Azerbaijani Turkic.
Writes the famous 'Şikâyetnâme' (The Book of Complaint), a satirical prose work protesting his not receiving a promised government stipend.
Dies during a plague outbreak in Karbala, where he was serving as a custodian at the shrine of Imam Husayn.

keyAchievements.title: Divans in Three Languages
keyAchievements.description: He composed extensive collections of poetry (divans) in Azerbaijani, Persian, and Arabic, showcasing his unparalleled linguistic and poetic mastery.

keyAchievements.title: Leylâ vü Mecnûn
keyAchievements.description: He authored the definitive Azerbaijani version of the classic Middle Eastern love story, which became a cornerstone of Turkic literature.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Azerbaijani Literary Language
keyAchievements.description: He elevated the Azerbaijani Turkic language to a high literary standard, profoundly influencing generations of poets across the Turkic world.

faq.question: Why did he choose the pen name 'Fuzûlî'?
faq.answer: He chose it because he believed no one else would want it, ensuring his uniqueness. The name has multiple meanings, including 'impertinent' or 'superfluous,' but can also imply a higher, virtuous quality, reflecting his complex artistic persona.

faq.question: What is his most famous work?
faq.answer: His most celebrated work is 'Leylâ vü Mecnûn' (Layla and Majnun), a poetic epic that tells the tragic story of a love that transcends the material world, blending earthly passion with Sufi mysticism.

faq.question: Was Fuzûlî a Sufi?
faq.answer: While not formally initiated into a specific Sufi order, his poetry is deeply infused with Sufi concepts, such as the annihilation of the self in the divine (fana), the pain of separation from the Beloved (God), and the use of love and wine as metaphors for spiritual ecstasy.

timeline.event: Born in Paj, a village near Tus in Khorasan, Sasanian Empire (modern-day Iran).
Begins composing the Shahnameh, his life's work, to preserve the history and mythology of Persia.
Completes the first version of the Shahnameh and presents it to Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, who famously underpays him.
Dies in his hometown of Tus, reportedly in poverty, but leaving behind a work that would define Persian culture.

keyAchievements.title: Authoring the Shahnameh
keyAchievements.description: He created the 'Shahnameh' (Book of Kings), the world's longest epic poem by a single author, which is central to Persian culture and identity.

keyAchievements.title: Revitalization of the Persian Language
keyAchievements.description: His near-exclusive use of Persian in the Shahnameh played a crucial role in reviving and standardizing the language after centuries of Arabic dominance.

faq.question: Why is the Shahnameh so important?
faq.answer: It is the national epic of Greater Iran, preserving its myths, pre-Islamic history, and language. It's considered a masterpiece of world literature and a cornerstone of Persian identity.

faq.question: Was Ferdowsi wealthy?
faq.answer: No. Despite his noble birth, he spent his family's fortune on his work and, according to legend, died in poverty after being slighted by his patron, Sultan Mahmud.

faq.question: How long is the Shahnameh?
faq.answer: It consists of over 50,000 rhyming couplets (distichs), making it more than twice the length of Homer's Iliad and Odyssey combined.

timeline.event: Born Ahmed Muhiddin Piri in Gallipoli, Ottoman Empire.
Begins his seafaring career, sailing with his uncle, the corsair and admiral Kemal Reis.
Compiles his first world map, famously known as the Piri Reis map, and presents it to Sultan Selim I.
Completes his 'Kitab-ı Bahriye' (Book of Navigation), a detailed atlas of the Mediterranean.
Creates his second world map, of which only a fragment showing Central and North America survives.
Appointed as the Reis (Admiral) of the Ottoman Fleet in the Indian Ocean.
Executed in Cairo on charges of treason and bribery following the siege of Hormuz.

keyAchievements.title: The Piri Reis Map of 1513
keyAchievements.description: One of the oldest surviving world maps to show the Americas, known for its remarkable detail and mysterious sources.

keyAchievements.title: Kitab-ı Bahriye (Book of Navigation)
keyAchievements.description: A comprehensive nautical atlas of the Mediterranean, containing detailed charts, port information, and sailing directions.

faq.question: How accurate is the Piri Reis map?
faq.answer: The map is remarkably accurate for its time regarding the coasts of Europe and North Africa. Its depiction of the Americas and a supposed southern continent (Antarctica) is a subject of intense debate and speculation among historians and cartographers.

faq.question: Why was Piri Reis executed?
faq.answer: He was executed on charges of treason and bribery after lifting the siege of the Portuguese-held Hormuz and returning to Egypt with his fleet, an act which his political enemies framed as desertion and corruption.

faq.question: What does 'Reis' mean?
faq.answer: 'Reis' is a Turkish title, equivalent to a captain, master, or admiral. Piri Reis's given name was Ahmed Muhiddin Piri.

timeline.event: Born in Rayy (in modern-day Iran).
Becomes the fifth Abbasid Caliph after the death of his brother al-Hadi.
Establishes or greatly expands the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad.
Launches a major campaign against the Byzantine Empire, reaching the Bosporus.
Divides the empire's succession between his sons, al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, setting the stage for future conflict.
Orders the arrest of the powerful Barmakid family, ending their influence over the court.
Dies in Tus (Khorasan, modern-day Iran) while on an expedition to quell a rebellion.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of the House of Wisdom
keyAchievements.description: Established the 'Bayt al-Hikma' in Baghdad, a grand library and translation institute that became the heart of the Islamic Golden Age.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural and Scientific Flourishing
keyAchievements.description: His reign saw unprecedented advancements in science, medicine, mathematics, and philosophy, attracting scholars from across the known world.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Relations with Charlemagne
keyAchievements.description: Initiated one of the first major diplomatic exchanges between the Islamic world and Western Europe, including the famous gift of an elephant named Abul-Abbas.

faq.question: Was Harun al-Rashid really like his character in 'One Thousand and One Nights'?
faq.answer: The tales are fictionalized and romanticized. While I was known for walking the streets of Baghdad in disguise to learn about my people, the magical adventures are literary creations that capture the opulence of my era.

faq.question: Wat was het Huis van Wijsheid?
faq.answer: It was a major intellectual center during my reign in Baghdad. It began as my personal library but grew into a public academy and translation hub where Greek, Persian, and Indian texts were translated into Arabic, preserving and expanding knowledge.

faq.question: Why did the Barmakid family fall from power?
faq.answer: The Barmakids, my trusted viziers, had amassed immense power and wealth. Their sudden downfall in 803 was likely due to my desire to consolidate my own authority and curb their influence, though the exact reasons remain a subject of historical debate.

timeline.event: Born Shams-ud-Din Mohammad Hafez-e Shirazi in Shiraz, Persia.
The Muzaffarid dynasty captures Shiraz, impacting Hafez's position and patronage at court.
Shah Shuja, a patron of poetry, restores Hafez to his position at court after a period of disfavor.
Falls from favor again and may have entered a period of exile from Shiraz.
Timur (Tamerlane) conquers Shiraz. A legendary, though likely apocryphal, meeting occurs between the poet and the conqueror.
Dies in Shiraz and is buried in the Musalla Gardens. His tomb is now a national monument and pilgrimage site.

keyAchievements.title: The Divan of Hafez
keyAchievements.description: His collection of poems, primarily in the ghazal form, is considered a pinnacle of Persian literature and a cultural touchstone.

keyAchievements.title: Mastery of the Ghazal
keyAchievements.description: He perfected the Persian lyric poetic form, the ghazal, infusing it with unprecedented mystical depth and layers of meaning.

keyAchievements.title: Enduring Cultural Icon
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is used for divination (Fal-e Hafez) and remains central to Iranian culture, education, and identity.

faq.question: What does the name 'Hafez' mean?
faq.answer: It is a pen name meaning 'The Memorizer' or 'The Guardian,' traditionally given to someone who has memorized the entire Quran by heart.

faq.question: Is his poetry about earthly love or divine love?
faq.answer: It's famously ambiguous. Terms like 'beloved,' 'wine,' and 'tavern' are interpreted as both literal, earthly pleasures and as Sufi mystical symbols for God, spiritual ecstasy, and a place of true worship.

faq.question: What is Fal-e Hafez?
faq.answer: It is a tradition of divination where Iranians, seeking guidance or insight, ask a question and then open Hafez's Divan to a random page, interpreting the poem as a response.

faq.question: Did Hafez really meet the conqueror Timur?
faq.answer: There is a famous legend about their meeting, where Hafez cleverly responds to Timur's criticism of his poetry. While a powerful story, most historians consider it to be apocryphal.

timeline.event: Born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire.
Graduated from the American College for Girls in Constantinople.
Divorced her first husband, Salih Zeki, over his decision to take a second wife.
Delivered her famous speech at the Sultanahmet demonstration against the Allied occupation.
Fled to Anatolia to join the Turkish National Movement; served in the Turkish War of Independence.
Lived in self-imposed exile in Europe and the United States due to political disagreements with the new government.
Returned to Turkey after Atatürk's death.
Elected as a member of the Grand National Assembly of Turkey.
Died in Istanbul.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering Turkish Novelist
keyAchievements.description: Authored influential novels like "The Shirt of Flame" and "The Clown and His Daughter," which explored themes of national identity and women's roles.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of the Turkish War of Independence
keyAchievements.description: Served as a corporal and sergeant on the front lines, becoming a powerful symbol of national resistance.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Feminist and Activist
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the first graduates of her college and a lifelong advocate for women's education and rights in Turkey.

keyAchievements.title: Member of Parliament
keyAchievements.description: Was elected to the Turkish Parliament in 1950, continuing her public service in the republic she helped create.

faq.question: Why is she considered a feminist icon?
faq.answer: She divorced her husband for attempting polygamy, advocated for women's education, and her novels featured strong, independent female characters, challenging the patriarchal norms of her time.

faq.question: Did she really fight as a soldier?
faq.answer: Yes. During the Turkish War of Independence, she was officially enlisted and served with the ranks of corporal and later sergeant, primarily in administrative and medical support roles near the front lines.

faq.question: Why did she go into exile after helping found the Republic?
faq.answer: She and her husband disagreed with what they saw as the increasingly authoritarian direction of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's government. They chose self-imposed exile to avoid political persecution.

timeline.event: Born in Babylon.
Ascends to the throne of Babylon after the abdication of his father, Sin-Muballit.
Conquers the cities of Isin and Uruk.
Defeats a coalition of enemies and conquers the powerful kingdom of Larsa, significantly expanding his empire.
Conquers the kingdom of Mari, securing control over most of Mesopotamia.
Establishes the Code of Hammurabi on a diorite stele, which is publicly displayed in Babylon.
Dies after a reign of 42 years, and is succeeded by his son, Samsu-iluna.

keyAchievements.title: Code of Hammurabi
keyAchievements.description: Created one of the world's earliest and most complete sets of written laws, establishing principles of justice and order for his empire.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Mesopotamia
keyAchievements.description: Transformed Babylon from a minor city-state into a dominant empire controlling the fertile crescent through military conquest and diplomacy.

keyAchievements.title: Public Works and Infrastructure
keyAchievements.description: Oversaw major projects including the expansion of canals for irrigation and the fortification of city walls, boosting agriculture and security.

faq.question: Was 'an eye for an eye' applied to everyone equally in the Code of Hammurabi?
faq.answer: No, the punishments varied based on the social status of the perpetrator and the victim. A crime against a nobleman received a harsher penalty than the same crime against a commoner or a slave.

faq.question: Was Hammurabi the first king to write down laws?
faq.answer: No, earlier law codes like the Code of Ur-Nammu existed. However, Hammurabi's code was far more comprehensive, systematic, and well-preserved, making it the most famous from the ancient Near East.

faq.question: What happened to Hammurabi's empire after his death?
faq.answer: The empire began to decline under his successor, Samsu-iluna. It faced internal revolts and external pressure, eventually shrinking and falling to invaders, but Hammurabi's cultural and legal legacy endured for centuries.

timeline.event: Born as Aleksandra Lisowska in Ruthenia, Kingdom of Poland (modern-day Ukraine).
Captured by Crimean Tatars and brought to Constantinople, entering the Imperial Harem of Sultan Suleiman.
Gives birth to her first son, Şehzade Mehmed, solidifying her status in the harem.
Suleiman frees and legally marries Hurrem, making her the Haseki Sultan, an unprecedented event.
Her rival, Grand Vizier Pargalı Ibrahim Pasha, is executed. Many historians believe she influenced this decision.
